strategic_overview: The best leverage is that the hypothesis is far stronger than it first looks: for a simple finite matroid, an order-reversing self-isomorphism of the flat lattice makes the opposite flat lattice geometric, which is essentially the modular/projective-geometric situation. This should be the first reduction, using Adjoints of Matroids, https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.08000, Proposition 4.1, Theorem 4.3, and Proposition 4.4. After that reduction, the most developed relative-Dressian technology is codimension one: Lorentzian polynomials and the incidence geometry of tropical linear spaces, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.12059, identifies Dr1 of a matroid with elementary valuated quotients, linear subclasses, and adjoints. That paper gives a very plausible point-hyperplane polarity layer, especially for modular matroids where Dr1_s(M) is isomorphic to Trop M.

directions.name: Mandatory reduction to modular self-adjoint matroids
directions.description: Before trying to construct Phi, reduce the hypothesis on L(M) to a structural classification of M. Work with the simplification of M, because the flat lattice ignores parallel elements and loops. Show that the existence of F -> F^perp implies that the simple matroid associated to L(M) is modular; then decompose it into connected projective-geometric components and rank-one components. Track separately how the given involution permutes components and how loops or parallel classes affect the relative Dressian.
key_ideas: An anti-automorphism L(M) -> L(M) identifies L(M)^op with a geometric lattice, which is the modularity signal.
After simplification, the connected components should be finite projective geometries or rank-one pieces; this massively narrows the problem.
If the problem is false, the counterexample must already occur in this highly structured modular/projective-geometric world, not in arbitrary matroids.
relevant_techniques: Adjoints of Matroids, https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.08000, Proposition 4.1: a simple matroid is modular iff it has an adjoint whose flat lattice is L(M)^op.

Adjoints of Matroids, https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.08000, Theorem 4.3 and Proposition 4.4: modular matroids have unique adjoints and decompose into finite projective geometries or one-element free components.
Adjoints of Matroids, https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.08000, Section 3 direct-sum results: adjoints preserve and reflect connected components; use this to reduce componentwise.

On local Dressians of matroids, https://arxiv.org/abs/1809.08965, Theorem 26: local Dressians factor over direct sums; while this is local rather than relative, the product-decomposition technique is useful.
Lorentzian polynomials and the incidence geometry of tropical linear spaces, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.12059, Proposition 6.15: elementary quotient lattices factor over direct sums.
directions.potential_obstacles: The flat lattice sees only the simplification, while Trop(M) and Dr(M) may remember loops, parallel coordinates, and boundary strata. Also, rank-3 projective planes need not be coordinatizable, so a purely linear-algebraic polarity argument may not cover every modular component.

directions.name: Build the codimension-one polarity first
directions.description: Construct and verify the desired duality only between tropical points of Trop(M) and codimension-one tropical subspaces of Trop(M). Use the simplified relative Dressian Dr1_s(M), with coordinates indexed by hyperplanes of M. The given flat-lattice involution identifies points with hyperplanes; combine this with the modular identification Dr1_s(M) ≅ Trop M to define a map p |-> H_p from points p in Trop(M) to relative hyperplanes H_p in Dr1(M). Check explicitly that a flat point coming from a hyperplane H is sent to the flat subspace corresponding to H^perp.
key_ideas: Any full polarity Phi must first solve the point-hyperplane problem.
For modular M, the codimension-one relative Dressian is already controlled by the same flat lattice as Trop(M).
This gives the most concrete place to force the prescribed extension F -> F^perp.
relevant_techniques: Lorentzian polynomials and the incidence geometry of tropical linear spaces, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.12059, Theorem 4.3: Dr1(mu) is cut out by explicit degenerate, parallel, and concurrent three-term incidence relations.

Lorentzian polynomials and the incidence geometry of tropical linear spaces, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.12059, Theorem 4.7: in the unvaluated case Dr1_s(M)^o is the order-complex fan of the lattice cQt1(M) of elementary quotients.

Lorentzian polynomials and the incidence geometry of tropical linear spaces, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.12059, Proposition 4.14(3): if M is modular, then W is unique and L(M) ≅ cQt1(M) ≅ L(W)^op, hence Dr1_s(M) ≅ Trop M.

Lorentzian polynomials and the incidence geometry of tropical linear spaces, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.12059, Proposition 5.6: if the underlying matroid is a direct sum of finite projective spaces, the simplified adjoint is unique and isomorphic to mu.

Matroid Polytopes, Nested Sets and Bergman Fans, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0411260, Theorem 4.4: nested cones decompose initial matroids into interval minors; useful for checking that the flat involution acts correctly on cones.
directions.potential_obstacles: This only treats rank-one versus corank-one. The isomorphism Dr1_s(M) ≅ Trop M may be noncanonical unless pinned down by the given polarity. One must also lift from simplified hyperplane coordinates back to the unsimplified Dr1(M), where bases of the same hyperplane are parallel coordinates with valuation shifts.

directions.name: Define Phi by a tropical Galois polar
directions.description: After constructing the point-hyperplane map p |-> H_p, define the candidate Phi on an arbitrary tropical subspace X ⊂ Trop(M) by the incidence polar Phi(X) = {p in Trop(M) : X ⊂ H_p}. Equivalently, try to realize Phi(X) as the stable intersection of the hyperplanes H_p for p in a finite tropical generating set of X. Then prove three things: Phi(X) is a tropical linear subspace of Trop(M), inclusion is reversed, and Phi(Phi(X)) = X. For flat subspaces this should reduce to the original lattice identity F -> F^perp.
key_ideas: Classical projective polarity is recovered by intersecting the polar hyperplanes of all points of a subspace.
The construction uses incidence only, so it is better adapted to relative Dressians than ambient Plucker-complement duality.
If it works, involutivity should be a Galois-closure statement: tropical linear subspaces are exactly the closed sets for this incidence relation.
relevant_techniques: Tropical Flag Varieties, https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.13727, Theorem 4.3.1: trop(mu) ⊆ trop(nu) iff mu is a valuated matroid quotient of nu; this is the main incidence-to-quotient dictionary.

Flag matroids with coefficients, https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.04658, Theorem 2.17 and Proposition 2.21: over the tropical hyperfield, flag matroids are chains of tropical linear spaces, i.e. chains of covector sets.

Tropical Linear Spaces, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0410455, Proposition 3.1 and Proposition 3.4: stable intersections are tropical linear spaces and lie inside set-theoretic intersections; use this to make finite hyperplane intersections tropical-linear.

Tropical Linear Spaces, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0410455, Theorem 3.6: stable intersection is the limit of generic translated intersections; useful if the incidence polar must be defined by a limiting process.

Lorentzian polynomials and the incidence geometry of tropical linear spaces, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.12059, Theorem D and Lemma 6.8: adjoints produce interpolating hyperplanes through prescribed points of Trop mu.

Representing Tropical Linear Spaces by Circuits, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0611579, circuit and cocircuit techniques, especially Lemma 15 and Theorem 16, suggest how to replace all points of X by a finite cocircuit or generator set in realizable cases.
directions.potential_obstacles: The set-theoretic intersection of tropical hyperplanes can be too large, while stable intersection can depend on choices unless handled carefully. It is not automatic that the polar of an arbitrary tropical linear subspace is again tropical linear. Double-polar equality may fail without a strong separation theorem for tropical linear subspaces inside Trop(M).

directions.name: Seek a valuated quotient formula for the relative orthogonal complement
directions.description: Translate the desired operation into valuated matroid quotient data. An element X of Dr(M) is a quotient mu_M -> theta. In classical linear algebra, the complement of W ⊂ V is not merely the ambient orthogonal W^perp; it is W^perp modulo V^perp, transported back to V by a nondegenerate polarity. Try to tropicalize exactly this relative quotient construction: dualize the flag theta ⊂ mu_M, remove the ambient dual part, and use the adjoint or flat polarity to return to a quotient of mu_M of complementary rank.
key_ideas: Ambient Hodge duality alone lands in the wrong space; the missing operation is quotienting by the dual of the ambient tropical linear space.
Flag Dressian duality gives the correct formal reversal of quotient chains.
Work first in adjacent ranks, where a quotient is deletion and contraction of a single valuated matroid on one extra element.
relevant_techniques: Tropical Flag Varieties, https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.13727, Proposition 4.2.3 and Theorem 4.3.1: valuated quotients are exactly tropical incidences and inclusions.
Flag matroids with coefficients, https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.04658, Proposition 2.11 and Theorem 2.13: duality reverses quotients and reverses flags.

Flag matroids with coefficients, https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.04658, Proposition 2.25: adjacent-rank flags over perfect tracts come from one-element minors; this is the best test case for a coordinate formula.

Tropical Flag Varieties, https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.13727, Theorem 5.1.2: adjacent-rank flag Dressians are obtained from an ordinary Dressian on one larger ground set, with a simple fiber description.

Matroidal Representations of Groups, https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.08674, Section 3.3 and Theorem 3.3.2: tropical Hodge star is valuated matroid duality and reverses subrepresentations when a pairing is available.

Self-dual matroids from canonical curves, https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.05910, Section 6 and Proposition 6.2: tropical Hodge star fixedness modulo lineality and cellwise duality in self-dual matroid subdivisions.
directions.potential_obstacles: The relative quotient by the ambient dual is not packaged in the literature as a single operation on valuated matroids. Duality changes rank relative to the full ground set n, whereas the desired Phi changes rank relative to rank(M). Non-realizable Dressian points may not admit a classical quotient-space construction, so the formula must be purely valuated or circuit-cocircuit based.

directions.name: Use small modular cases and finite quotient subposets to find obstructions
directions.description: In parallel with construction attempts, test whether an anti-involution can exist by comparing complementary-rank layers and intervals in examples forced by the reduction: U_{n,n}, U_{2,n}, Fano and non-Desargues projective planes, PG(3,2), and direct sums of these. Start with the subposet of trivial valuated quotients, then include elementary valuated quotients using Dr1_s(M). Look for invariants that an anti-automorphism would preserve: cardinalities of finite quotient intervals, existence of common upper or lower quotients, dimensions of layers, and incidence graphs between points and hyperplanes.
key_ideas: A global anti-involution would identify the rank-k and rank-(r-k) relative Dressian layers as posets.
The elementary layer is computable via linear subclasses; higher layers can be attacked with incidence equations.
Counterexamples, if they exist under the flat-polarity hypothesis, are likely to show up first as asymmetry of quotient intervals rather than as failure of the flat lattice.
relevant_techniques: Algorithms for Tight Spans and Tropical Linear Spaces, https://arxiv.org/abs/1612.03592, Algorithm 1 and closure-system techniques: use these to enumerate face posets and Hasse diagrams in small examples.
Tropical Incidence Relations, Polytopes, and Concordant Matroids, https://arxiv.org/abs/1211.2841, Theorem 1: incidence relations give explicit equations for containment of tropical linear spaces.

Lorentzian polynomials and the incidence geometry of tropical linear spaces, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.12059, Example 6.14 and Proposition 6.16: quotient posets can fail upper semimodularity in rank at least 4; use this as a warning and source of finite obstruction patterns.

On Adjoints and Dual Matroids, https://ris.utwente.nl/ws/files/133448319/adjoints.pdf, Corollary 2.2 and Proposition 3.1: Cheung-style Levi obstruction gives a template for proving nonexistence from finite incidence collapse.

The tropical Grassmannian, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0304218, duality observation after Theorem 3.8: U_{n,n} has the standard ambient Hodge anti-involution, so nonsemimodularity alone is not an obstruction.
directions.potential_obstacles: A poset anti-involution need not preserve a chosen fan structure, so f-vectors alone are weak evidence. Some finite quotient-poset pathologies occur already for U_{n,n}, where ambient Hodge duality nevertheless gives an anti-involution. Computations beyond rank 3 or 4 may become large unless symmetry and simplification are exploited.

key_obstacles: The main barrier is that the known universal order-reversing operation, tropical Hodge duality, is ambient: it sends a subspace of tropical projective n-space to a subspace of the dual ambient space. The problem asks for a relative complement inside Trop(M), so one must quotient out the ambient dual of Trop(M) and then use the given flat polarity to return to Trop(M). That relative operation is not currently a standard valuated-matroid construction. The second barrier is that codimension one is well understood through elementary quotients and adjoints, but higher-rank relative Dressian layers are much less explicit. The third barrier is non-realizability: classical polarity or linear-algebra arguments may describe the tropical Grassmannian part but not the full relative Dressian. Finally, loops, parallel classes, and projective boundary strata can obscure the clean modular picture supplied by the flat lattice.
recommended_starting_point: Start with the reduction and the codimension-one polarity. The very first step should be: replace M by its simplification and prove, using Adjoints of Matroids, https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.08000, Proposition 4.1, that the flat-lattice involution forces the simplification to be modular. Then fix the induced bijection between points and hyperplanes and write the explicit map Trop(M) -> Dr1_s(M) using Lorentzian polynomials and the incidence geometry of tropical linear spaces, https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.12059, Proposition 4.14(3). Verify on the dense cones corresponding to chains of flats that flat points are sent exactly to the prescribed F^perp flat subspaces. Only after this point-hyperplane layer is completely pinned down should solver agents attempt the full polar Phi(X) = {p : X is contained in H_p}.
